text.content:
Cindren & Vhal — Hardby branch. The chops the party wants are inside; the question is which path opens the door.

The building

A two-story counting house on Counting House Lane, in the merchants' quarter. Pale limestone with dark wood trim, a brass plate beside the door reading CINDREN & VHAL — BONDED FACTORS — HARDBY BRANCH, a small green-glass window above the door bearing the firm's crane-and-coins seal. Two stories above ground, one story below; the lower level holds the vault and a small private interview room.

The branch handles, on its public face, ordinary trade-clearing for Yeomanry-Aerdi commerce: bonded shipping escrow, credit on cargo holds, currency clearing between regions. On its private face — the side the party is here to investigate — it handles the operational accounting of the conspiracy's eastern movements.

Hours and access

Open to the public second through eighth bell after dawn, six days of the week (closed Festday). Senior staff present until tenth bell most evenings. After hours the building is locked and a single guard (a former harbor-watch corporal named Dren Marsh) sleeps in a small back room off the foyer.

Standing staff (12 total)

  • Mira Cindren — junior partner, Veshen Cindren's cousin, mid-forties. Public face of the branch; receives all formal visits. Polite, careful, well-prepared, and gives the party absolutely nothing useful by herself. She is not in the conspiracy directly; she manages the public ledgers and is kept ignorant of the second set so that her plausible deniability remains intact. (GM: she is recoverable as a Phase 4 witness if the conspiracy collapses around her.)
  • Solen Mereth — head of the Hardby Bond-Loading Office, late forties. The compromised senior clerk. Operates with independent authority on bond-loading, which means he can write countersignatures and apply chops without Mira's knowledge. He is the man who chopped the marginal endorsement on Haskur's escrow. See his own page below.
  • Hesren Vesh — recently recalled from Loftwick (only present if Phase 2's Pressure Clock segment 5 fired). Now a senior clerk on paper; in practice has no responsibilities and is being slow-walked toward dismissal or worse. See Hardby NPCs → Hesren Vesh — Recalled to Hardby.
  • Eight other clerks and bond-officers of varying seniority. None are in the conspiracy. Most are competent civic functionaries who would be horrified to learn what Solen has been doing. Three of them are quietly worried about the second set of books they have heard rumors of but have not seen.
  • One vault clerk, Halver Onn, mid-fifties, a former Aerdi treasury clerk who came to Hardby twenty years ago. Quiet, methodical, has not yet realized that the "special escrow" ledger Solen asks him to file in the side cabinet is not properly registered. He is not corrupt; he is incurious in the way that lifelong clerks become incurious as a survival strategy.
  • Dren Marsh — night guard, former harbor-watch corporal, sleeps in the back room off the foyer. He is bribable for small information (the schedule of senior staff arrivals) but not for entry; he honors his employment.

What's in the building

Ground floor

  • Foyer. Brass plate, polished slate floor, two visitors' chairs, a desk where the duty clerk receives walk-in business. The duty clerk rotates among the eight junior staff.
  • Public counter-room. Long counter, three clerks' positions, a wall of pigeonhole filing for current public business. This is where Mira Cindren receives an official delegation if one arrives.
  • Mira's office. Off the counter-room, a small private chamber with a desk, two visitors' chairs, a sideboard of decanters, the firm's annual reports for the last decade in a glass-fronted cabinet.
  • Solen Mereth's bond-loading office. Off the back of the counter-room, a separate room with its own door — he insisted on this when he took the post. Inside: a tall standing desk, a wall of bond-loading files, a tall locked cabinet against the back wall (the second set of books lives here), and a small private cipher-key safe behind the cabinet (Solen keeps the key on his belt at all times). His office has a back window onto the alley; the window is barred but the bars are old.
  • Back foyer / staff entrance. Door to the alley. Marsh's small back room (cot, lantern, kettle, a chair).

Second floor

  • Senior partner's office. Standing empty. Tarlith Vhal Sereth has not visited Hardby in eleven years; the office is kept dusted for the appearance of presence. The party can search it without finding much, but: the desk's blotter holds an old impression of a letter dictated to Solen's hand and signed in another hand (Tarlith Vhal Sereth's) that, if rubbed up with charcoal, gives a partial signature comparable to other documents.
  • Conference room. A long table for partner meetings. Used twice yearly. Otherwise unremarkable.
  • Senior staff offices. Three small offices for the senior clerks (including Solen and the recently-arrived Hesren). Each holds private correspondence and personal files.
  • Strongroom. Smaller than the vault below. Holds current senior correspondence, the firm's seals (other than the public seal kept downstairs), and the firm's master copies of its branch chops. The defect-stamp Veska identified — the Hardby clearing-stamp Solen was supposed to have destroyed in 579 CY — is in this strongroom, not the lower vault. The strongroom door has a single lock; Mira and Solen each have a key.

Lower level

  • The vault. Stone-walled, vaulted ceiling, a single iron-banded door with two locks (Mira and Halver Onn each hold one). Inside: the firm's bonded escrow holdings (substantial cash and credit notes), the public ledgers of every transaction the branch has processed for the last twenty years (bound, dated, indexed), and the side cabinet Solen instructs Halver to file the "special escrow" in. The side cabinet contains the second set of books.
  • Private interview room. Off the vault corridor, a small soundproofed chamber with a single table and two chairs. Used for sensitive client meetings. Currently empty most days.

The smoking gun

What the party needs to recover from the building, in priority order:

  1. The second set of books from Solen's side cabinet in the vault. This documents the conspiracy's transactions in the firm's own ink, signed off by Solen at branch level. Roughly 18 months of entries, ~140 transactions.
  2. The senior partner's signature impression from the upstairs blotter. Combined with the second-set entries, this lets Veska prepare a notarial opinion that Tarlith Vhal Sereth personally authorized at least three of the transactions. This is what gets a senior partner indicted, not just a branch clerk.
  3. The defect-stamp from the upstairs strongroom. Physical proof that the Hardby branch retained — and used — a clearing-stamp it had officially destroyed.
  4. Solen's private cipher key from the safe behind his office cabinet (key on his belt). Unlocks ciphered annotations in the second set, which name three further transactions and one additional Rel Astra correspondent.

Items 1–3 alone are sufficient for a Yeomanry or Aerdi indictment of Solen Mereth and the Hardby branch. Item 1 plus item 2 names Tarlith Vhal Sereth. Item 4 is a bonus that gives the party Phase 4 leads.

Three ways in

The party gets the documents one of three ways, depending on travel approach and play style:

  • Burglary (Quiet Pursuit only). Break in at night. Vault has two locks (DC 17 each, or both keys). Solen's office cabinet has a single lock (DC 16). Strongroom has a single lock (DC 16). Time inside before the night guard's third-bell round: about 90 minutes. The party can be in and out in one night if they are competent and prepared. See The Burglary page.
  • Pretexted access (Commercial Cover). Two or three visits as wool-trade clients let the party observe the building, learn staff schedules, identify which clerk is sympathetic (one of the three quietly-worried clerks will be approachable). Combined with one carefully-timed pretexted visit during a senior-staff meeting, the party can get a sympathetic clerk to copy-read a few entries from the second set. This yields paper evidence (Veska's notarial opinion based on the copy-reading) that is admissible in Aerdi court but slightly weaker than the originals. See The Sympathetic Clerk page.
  • Formal subpoena (Official Delegation). The accredited delegation can, with three days' procedural delay and two of Caelith's last political favors burned, file a formal subpoena for branch records under Aerdi commercial-disclosure law. The firm complies. The records they produce are, of course, the public ledgers — which contain nothing useful — but the act of subpoena lets the party search the senior partner's office (the upstairs blotter) and the strongroom. This gets them items 2 and 3 but not item 1. See The Subpoena page.

All three paths produce a viable Phase 4 indictment package. The Quiet Pursuit burglary path produces the strongest package; the Commercial Cover path produces a slightly weaker but still court-ready package; the Official Delegation subpoena path produces the weakest package but the cleanest legal standing for the party.

Tactical Map

C&V Hardby branch — tactical map. Solen's office is the northeast corner, the strongroom in the north wall.

Solen Mereth, senior clerk. He has been signing what he is told to sign for almost three years.

Who he is

Solen Mereth, late forties, Aerdi-born, head of the Hardby Bond-Loading Office at Cindren & Vhal Hardby for nine years. Came to the firm from a junior position at a Rel Astra counting house in 1573; promoted steadily; given the Hardby Bond-Loading head position in 1574 and given a quiet additional brief in 1580 by Tarlith Vhal Sereth personally — to keep a second set of books on a small group of transactions Sereth would name to him.

Solen accepted the brief. He has, since 1580, run the second set of books with steady professional competence. He believes (or has decided to believe) that the second set documents legitimate but politically sensitive transactions that the firm needs to keep separate from public ledgers for Aerdi imperial-tax reasons. He has not asked too many questions about what the transactions actually are. He is not a fanatic; he is a man who took a senior brief from a senior partner and has done the brief well.

Description

Tall, lean, balding, careful clean-shaven features. Wears the firm's senior-clerk livery (dark green wool coat, brass crane-and-coins pin, a small additional pin denoting head-of-office). Reading lenses on a black silk cord. Speaks in short measured sentences. Watches everyone who enters his office.

Where he is

His office at the back of the counter-room, ground floor. Arrives at the second bell after dawn, leaves after the tenth bell of evening. He takes the noon meal at his desk. His own residence is a small house in the merchants' quarter, three streets from the branch.

Schedule

Predictable. Second bell after dawn — arrives. Third bell — opens the bond-loading day. Fourth bell — first courier arrives, checks the morning's loading bonds. Fifth bell — Mira Cindren passes through to coordinate the day's appointments. Sixth bell — first private hour; Solen often closes his office door for thirty minutes during this hour to work on the second set undisturbed. Seventh through tenth bell — public business. After tenth bell — leaves.

Festdays — does not come to the office. Spends the day at home with his wife (Inella, a milliner) and their two adult sons (one a junior officer in the Hardby city watch, one a clerk in a different counting house).

His weakness

Solen is professionally proud. He believes himself to be a competent senior clerk who has handled a difficult brief well. If the party can present him, in a private and procedurally correct setting, with evidence that the transactions in the second set are not what he believed they were, he can be turned. Not turned to active assistance — he will not betray the firm — but turned to a slow careful resignation. He will go home. He will not destroy the second set on his way out. He will not warn Sereth. He will write a single sealed letter to his eldest son explaining where to find what is in the office, in case the eldest son's watch career benefits from the information.

This is a long approach and requires the party to play it carefully across multiple days. It is most viable on the Commercial Cover path. The Persuasion DC for the turn is high (DC 22 with notarized evidence in hand from Veska, modified by relevant roleplay), but the consequence of success is significant: the party gets the second set delivered to their lodgings, by a sealed packet, the morning after Solen resigns.

His strength

If the party tries to confront him without a procedurally clean evidence package, or tries to bully him, or tries to suborn him with money or threat, Solen does the right thing for a man in his position: he says nothing, ends the meeting, and writes immediately to Tarlith Vhal Sereth in Rel Astra. The firm hardens. The Pressure Clock for Phase 3 advances by two segments. Tamsin (if alive) is moved further. Tarsh (if available) is recalled. The party loses the soft path.

Combat

Solen is not a combatant. He carries no weapon. If the party assaults him, he yields immediately and survives the assault — but the party loses every legal-standing benefit of the case they were building. Solen Mereth is most useful to the party as a man left alive.

Stat block

Use a SRD Noble with no weapon and Persuasion +6 / Insight +4 / Investigation +6. He will not roll initiative.

Path 1 — The burglary. Quiet, fast, no fight.

Setup

The party breaks into the Cindren & Vhal Hardby branch by night. This path is most viable for a party on the Quiet Pursuit travel approach (the firm has not yet hardened the building) but can be attempted by Commercial Cover parties at higher difficulty.

Approach

The building has three exterior approaches:

  • The front door on Counting House Lane. Locked, two locks (DC 16 each). Visible from the lane; the watch passes every twenty minutes, but the lane is otherwise quiet after the eleventh bell.
  • The staff door at the back of the building, opening on the alley. Locked, one lock (DC 16). Marsh sleeps just inside this door — opening it without waking him requires Stealth DC 14 (his hearing is fading; he is not a hard sleeper but he is not a vigilant one).
  • Solen's office window. Second floor of the back wall — wait, ground-floor of the back wall. Bars on the window are old (DC 14 to bend or remove silently with leverage). The window is unlocked from inside.

Inside

The building is dark; the only lit area is Marsh's small back room (single oil lamp, kept low). The party has roughly 90 minutes between Marsh's last round of the foyer (eleventh bell) and his next round (third bell after midnight). After his third-bell round he sleeps until dawn.

Targets

1. Solen's office cabinet (the second set of books) — Required.

The tall locked cabinet against the back wall of Solen's office. Single lock (DC 16) plus a small needle-tipped poison trap on the latch (DC 15 Investigation to spot, DC 14 Dex save to disarm; on failure: a needle pricks the picker's finger and they take 4d4 poison damage and are Poisoned for 1 hour). Inside: the second set of books, bound in three volumes, ~18 months of entries. Each volume is the size of a slim hymnal; the three together fit in a courier's wallet. Take the books.

2. The vault side cabinet — Required if the office cabinet is empty.

If the books are not in Solen's cabinet (sometimes he files them in the vault for safekeeping), they are in the side cabinet of the vault. The vault has two locks (DC 17 each). The party will need either both keys (Mira and Halver each carry one) or Pick Locks DC 17 twice. Inside the vault, the side cabinet has its own lock (DC 15) but no trap.

3. The senior partner's blotter — Required for Sereth's signature.

Upstairs, the senior partner's office. The blotter holds an old impression of a Sereth-signed letter. A piece of charcoal rubbed across the blotter yields a partial signature. Take the entire blotter (it is a slim leather folio — fits in a satchel) so Veska can rub it up cleanly.

4. The defect-stamp — Required for the chop chain of evidence.

Upstairs, the strongroom. Strongroom door: single lock (DC 16). Inside: the firm's master chops in a velvet-lined case. The defect-stamp is the third stamp from the left. Take it. Replace it with the second-from-left stamp shifted into its place if the party wants to delay discovery; otherwise the firm notices the missing stamp the next time anyone opens the case (typically every third day).

5. Solen's cipher-key safe — Optional but valuable.

Behind the cabinet in Solen's office, a small wall safe. Solen carries the key on his belt at all times. Without the key: DC 19 to crack. Inside: a small black notebook of cipher keys, including the cipher used in the second set's marginal annotations. This is the Phase 4 lead — it names a Rel Astra correspondent the party would not otherwise identify.

Complications

  • Marsh wakes early. If the party makes more than one mistake (failed Stealth check, dropped lantern, loud lockpick), Marsh wakes for his second-bell round instead of his third-bell round. He is armed (a short sword) and competent (Veteran statline) but not eager to die for his employer. If the party identifies themselves as Yeomanry inquirers and shows Caelith's brass token, he will set down the sword and walk back to his room. He will, however, write his shift report honestly in the morning.
  • The cipher trap. If the party takes the second set without taking Solen's cipher key, half the marginal annotations in the books are unreadable. They have evidence of transactions but not all of the names. The Phase 4 indictment is still viable but the Phase 4 investigation is harder.
  • Footprints in the strongroom dust. The strongroom is dusted twice a year. The party's bootprints will be visible until the next dusting (about three months). When the firm discovers the burglary, this is what they look at first to determine which strongroom items were touched.

After

The firm discovers the burglary at the second bell after dawn when Solen opens his office cabinet and finds it empty. Within three hours, every senior staff member knows. Within six hours, Sereth has been notified by sealed courier. Within two days, the firm understands what was taken and assumes (correctly) that the party is in Hardby.

The Pressure Clock for Phase 3 (see Departure) advances by 3 segments on a successful burglary. The party should plan to leave Hardby within 48 hours of the break-in.

Vella Tannin. She has noticed; she has not yet decided what to do about it.

Setup

The party works as wool-trade visitors over multiple days, observes the branch, identifies a clerk who can be approached, and gets that clerk to copy-read entries from the second set in a private setting. This path is most viable on the Commercial Cover travel approach.

The clerk

Vella Tannin, mid-thirties, junior bond-officer at the Hardby branch for six years. One of the three clerks who have heard the rumors of a second set of books and are quietly worried about them. She lost a brother in the Wild Coast border skirmishes of 580 — he was a Yeomanry-aligned merchant who was killed when his cargo "disappeared" under bonded escrow that turned out to have been falsified. She has never connected her brother's death to the firm. The party can.

Vella is approachable in three ways: she takes lunch most days at a small bread-shop two streets from the branch (alone, eats while reading), she attends Castrian Vell's salon once every six weeks (Castrian sponsored her brother's small bookseller's debt years ago and has known her since), and she walks home alone after the eighth bell evening through a quiet stretch of the merchants' quarter.

The approach

This is a slow-burn approach that requires the party to spend time. Three to five Hardby days, depending on play. The conversation arc:

  • First contact. Casually, at the bread-shop or at Castrian's salon. The party is wool-trade visitors interested in Hardby commerce. Vella is friendly, careful. She does not yet trust them.
  • Building rapport. A second meeting, again casual. Reveal something small but real — that the party knows Hardby's brokers buy from people they should not, that the public ledgers do not fully match the bonded shipping logs, that someone in Loftwick has been quietly worried for a year. Watch how she reacts. Insight DC 14: she is interested but afraid. Insight DC 17: her brother is the lever.
  • The lever. If the party identifies her brother's death (Veska can help cross-reference; the public Yeomanry trade-clearing record of the lost cargo is in the records) and brings it to her — privately, gently, with respect — she breaks. She has been waiting eight years for someone to confirm what she has not let herself believe.
  • The copy-read. Vella will, on a single carefully-chosen evening, take Castrian's house key (which she can borrow on legitimate salon-night business), and bring carbon copies of one week's worth of second-set entries to the party's lodging. She will read them aloud while the party transcribes. She cannot take the originals — Solen counts the volumes nightly — but she can carbon-copy the entries during her sixth-bell hour when Solen leaves his office to do the day's reconciliation with Mira.

Yield

One week of carbon-copied second-set entries (approximately 18 transactions). Names, sums, dates, the Sereth signature pattern in two of them. This is enough for Veska's notarial opinion to identify Tarlith Vhal Sereth. It is enough for an Aerdi or Yeomanry court indictment of Solen and the Hardby branch.

It is not enough to recover the defect-stamp (still in the upstairs strongroom — but Vella can tell the party where it is) or to confirm the cipher key in Solen's safe (Vella does not have access to Solen's office). The Phase 4 leads about Rel Astra are weaker on this path.

What the party owes Vella

Two things, both important.

  • Extraction. Vella has just signed her own death warrant by carbon-copying. The party must have her out of Hardby within 24 hours. Castrian can hide her overnight; a coastal galley to Saltmarsh leaves at dawn; she has cousins there who will receive her without questions. The party should pay her passage and provide a small purse. If the party leaves Hardby without extracting Vella, she is murdered within ten days.
  • Honest indictment. Vella has trusted the party with the truth about her brother. The Phase 4 indictment must, when it is filed, name him as a victim. The party can promise this; Caelith will honor it.

Failure modes

If the party rushes Vella, threatens her, offers her money to copy directly without earning her trust, she withdraws. The party loses this path entirely. Vella reports the contact to no one — she is not loyal to the firm — but she will not work with the party. The party must fall back to the burglary path (much harder under Commercial Cover) or the subpoena path (only viable for Official Delegation parties, which Commercial Cover parties are not).

Mira Cindren. She will receive the official delegation, and she will give you nothing.

Setup

The accredited delegation files a formal subpoena under Aerdi commercial-disclosure law for the firm's branch records related to specific named transactions. This path is available only on the Official Delegation travel approach. It produces the cleanest legal standing of the three paths but the weakest evidence package, because the firm complies with the subpoena's letter (public ledgers) while the second-set entries remain hidden.

The procedural cost

Filing the subpoena requires:

  • The Yeomanry councilman heading the delegation to formally request it from the Hardby Office of Mercantile Records (Zoria Weis's office). The councilman will do this if the party requests it through proper channels and accepts that this burns two of Caelith's last political favors with the public-closure faction back in Loftwick.
  • Three days of Aerdi procedural delay between filing and execution. During this delay, the firm has time to sanitize the public ledgers further (they have already done this to the extent they could) and to prepare prepared answers from Mira Cindren.
  • An accompanying writ for "senior officer interview," which lets the party question Mira Cindren under formal conditions for up to one hour each day for three days.

What the firm produces

On the morning of the subpoena's execution: forty-three bound volumes of public ledgers covering the requested time period and transactions. Mira Cindren herself receives the party in the conference room and walks them through the records personally. Coffee is served. The records are exactly what they appear to be — clean, complete, internally consistent, and useless.

Mira's interviews are similarly polished. She answers every question fully. She volunteers nothing. She is genuinely cooperative; she is also genuinely uninformed about the second set. The party will leave the interviews knowing more about the firm's public business than they ever wanted to know and nothing about the conspiracy.

What the subpoena lets the party do that they could not otherwise

This is the value of the path: during execution, the subpoena gives the party legal access to specific rooms in the building.

  • The senior partner's office. The party may, under the subpoena, search the office to confirm absence of relevant correspondence. They may not seize anything — but they may take a charcoal rubbing of the blotter, which is what they actually need.
  • The strongroom (with restrictions). The party may inspect the firm's chops to verify chop chains in the public records. They may not seize. But — if the party carefully notes the contents of the chop case — the third stamp from the left is missing the small chip Veska described. The defect-stamp is here. The party can identify it by sight in the case. They can demand a notarized record of all chops present, which Mira must produce. That notarized record, comparing the present-day stamp to Veska's earlier records, names the discrepancy.
  • The vault. The party may inspect the public ledger storage. They may also note the side cabinet (which is locked and which Mira will explain is "sealed escrow under client privilege") and demand a notarized inventory of its existence. The cabinet's existence is itself evidence — Aerdi commercial law does not permit branch-level "sealed escrow" outside the partner-level vault, which is in Rel Astra.

Yield

  • The blotter rubbing (Sereth's signature impression).
  • A notarized record of chop discrepancy (the defect-stamp issue).
  • A notarized record of an improper sealed-escrow cabinet at branch level.

Combined with Veska's prior records and the Phase 1/2 evidence, this is enough for an Aerdi court to issue a warrant against the Hardby branch and a writ of partner accountability against Tarlith Vhal Sereth. The party leaves Hardby with a court-issued warrant in their satchel. That warrant is the cleanest legal weapon they will carry into Rel Astra in Phase 4.

It is also the slowest, the most expensive politically, and the least personally satisfying. The party never sees the second set of books. They never read a Sereth-signed transaction in the firm's own ink. They will, however, see Tarlith Vhal Sereth in person in Phase 4, with a warrant in hand, which the other two paths do not guarantee.

The Tamsin question

On this path, Tamsin Moraven is already dead by the time the party arrives. The subpoena does not help with her. The party may, separately, file for a coroner's inquiry on her body — Mira Cindren will not obstruct this; she has no reason to — and recover Tamsin's last letter, which a sympathetic warehouse worker named Hella Voren (Veska's former apprentice) is holding for the right person to ask. That letter is Tamsin's voice still speaking to her family. The party should bring it home.
